questions.retrievalTag: Acute trauma dissociative disorder
questions.stem: A 32-year-old Bosnian man is brought to the emergency department by police 30 minutes after they found him sitting in a cemetery and muttering to himself. The police officers report that the patient appeared lost in thought and did not respond to their questions; he then passively accompanied them to the hospital. Identification in the patient's wallet indicates that he is a construction worker. The physician calls the patient's employer and learns that he was part of a crew working on a bridge that morning when a crane collapsed, causing multiple injuries to people working in trailers on the ground. A hospital interpreter says that the patient had been an ambulance driver in a military war zone 10 years ago. The patient sits quietly and stares ahead; he appears unaffected by the bustling activity in the emergency department. His pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a reserved demeanor. He speaks in monotone and responds to questions with vague, noncommittal answers. He says he is not depressed or anxious. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Dissociative disorders include dissociative identity disorder, dissociative amnesia, and depersonalization- derealization disorder. These occur when consciousness, memory, identity, or awareness of self and surroundings are disrupted and are frequently precipitated by acute trauma.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Dissociative disorder occurs when consciousness, memory, identity, or awareness of self and surroundings are disrupted. Depersonalization, detachment from one's self, and derealization, the sense of the world being strange or unreal, are also features. Within the umbrella of dissociative disorder are several subtypes: dissociative identity disorder, dissociative amnesia, and depersonalization-derealization disorder. Acute trauma, such as in this case, is a characteristic cause of dissociative amnesia, depersonalization, and derealization. Chronic trauma, severe stress, substance abuse, and underlying psychiatric disorders are additional potential triggers. When dissociation occurs acutely secondary to trauma, it also frequently remits acutely. If it does persist, however, the cornerstone of treatment is psychotherapy. If psychotherapy is not effective, an atypical antipsychotic or antiepileptic such as lamotrigine may be used.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) is characterized by anxiety, depressed mood, or inappropriate behavior in response to a profound life change. Adjustment disorder is only diagnosed when the patient does not meet the diagnostic criteria for another psychiatric disorder. In this case, the patient meets criteria for dissociative disorder instead. Catatonia (Choice B) can occur in the context of many underlying psychiatric disorders. Its key characteristic is a motor disturbance and inability to move normally, despite having the physical capacity to do so. It may also present with mutism, stupor, staring, or posturing. This patient does not demonstrate any motor disturbances, making a diagnosis of catatonia unlikely. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice D) requires the presence of excessive worry about numerous aspects of daily life occurring on more days than not for at least 6 months, with a consequent inability to control symptoms of anxiety, and with the presence of at least three other symptoms (irritability, muscle tension, sleep problems, poor concentration, fatigue, or restlessness). These symptoms are absent in this patient. Schizotypal personality disorder (Choice E) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster. The disorder is characterized by odd behavior and thinking and a constricted affect. Patients with schizotypal personality disorder may have strange, overly metaphorical or magical thinking, though they fluctuate in the degree of their conviction about these beliefs. This patient does not demonstrate these symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: 47-Year-Old 2-Day History Intense Anxiety Been Receiving Chemotherapy
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of intense anxiety. He has been receiving chemotherapy for hepatoma for 5 days. His nausea is well controlled with prochlorperazine. He has no history of similar symptoms or psychiatric illness. His pulse is 104/min, and blood pressure is 142/86 mm Hg. Mental status examination shows an anxious affect. He paces, wrings his hands, and says, "I feel as though I am going to jump out of my skin!" Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Antidopaminergic medications commonly lead to extrapyramidal symptoms, including akathisia. Akathisia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome featuring inner restlessness that may be associated with intense anxiety and a compulsion to move.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Prochlorperazine is a first-generation antipsychotic medication that is commonly utilized as an antiemetic. Its antidopaminergic activity may lead to extrapyramidal symptoms (eg, acute dystonic reaction, parkinsonism, or akathisia). This patient is likely experiencing akathisia, a neuropsychiatric syndrome featuring inner restlessness that may be associated with intense anxiety and a compulsion to move. The pathophysiology of akathisia is incompletely understood but may be related to an imbalance in dopaminergic and cholinergic activity (similar to other extrapyramidal symptoms). Management typically includes β-adrenergic blockers or benzodiazepines, though anticholinergic medications (eg, benztropine) are second-line treatments. Alternatively, the offending agent may be decreased or discontinued.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, D, E and F. Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance of trauma reminders, and/or negative mood or thoughts that arise within 1 month of a traumatic event such as a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. This patient does not have a history of a traumatic event, intrusive thoughts, avoidance, or negative alterations in mood or cognition. Adjustment disorder (Choice B) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with depression or anxiety symptoms. This patient's anxiety does not appear to be related to a stressor but instead driven by inner restlessness. Anxiety disorder due to hepatoma (Choice D) refers to anxiety that is secondary to physiologic changes related to a hepatoma (eg, increased systemic inflammation). This patient's psychomotor r,estlessness that correlated with the initiation of prochlorperazine is more consistent with akathisia. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice E) is characterized by 6 months or more of excessive worrying about a range of topics and is associated with three additional symptoms (irritability, muscle tension, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, fatigability, or sleep disturbance). This patient's anxiety is related to inner restlessness rather than excessive worrying. Panic disorder (Choice F) is an anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with four additional physical symptoms (eg, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, or tremulousness) or associated mental states (eg, fear of dying).

questions.retrievalTag: 72-Year-Old Woman 3-Month History Fatigue Difficulty Sleeping 5.4-Kg
questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of fatigue and difficulty sleeping. She has had a 5.4-kg (12-lb) weight loss during this period because of decreased appetite. Her husband died 18 months ago. She has a 15-year history of hypertension treated with verapamil and a 3-month history of insomnia treated with zolpidem. Laboratory studies 1 week ago showed no abnormalities. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 60 kg (133 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Her pulse is 74/min, and blood pressure is 131/84 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an irritable and tearful mood. She expresses hopelessness about the future. Which of the following is the most likely finding in this patient?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Increased percentage of bands on complete blood count

questions.educationalObjective: Major depressive disorder (MDD) is characterized by depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation and consequent hypercortisolemia is postulated to be involved in the cause of MDD.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. Though some patients are able to express a depressed mood, others (particularly women, elderly patients, and African American patients) may primarily endorse somatic or neurovegetative symptoms. MDD is postulated to result from dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, particularly in response to stress, and consequent hypercortisolemia (increased serum cortisol concentration). Cortisol promotes changes in serotonin receptors, mediating mood changes. Treatment of MDD should include psychotherapy and antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Decreased hemoglobin concentration (Choice A), anemia, is commonly associated with fatigue. However, difficulty sleeping, appetite disturbances, and tearful affect are more consistent with MDD. Decreased serum vitamin 8 1 (thiamine) concentration (Choice B) is associated with Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, a neurologic syndrome characterized by impaired anterograde and retrograde memory. This patient's tearful affect and neurovegetative symptoms are more consistent with MDD. Increased percentage of bands on complete blood count (Choice C) refers to a proliferation of banded neutrophils in response to infection. This patient does not possess vital sign abnormalities or focal signs on physical examination consistent with infection. An increased serum prolactin concentration (Choice E) can result from a prolactinoma or an antidopaminergic medication. This patient is not taking an antidopaminergic medication and does not endorse symptoms of a pituitary prolactinoma (eg, galactorrhea, headaches, vision changes).

questions.retrievalTag: 52-Year-Old Homeless Alcoholism Emergency Department Police After Being
questions.stem: A 52-year-old homeless man with alcoholism is brought to the emergency department by police after being found agitated and confused. He says he feels nervous and could use a drink. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. He is trembling. His hematocrit is 30%. Urinalysis shows 7 WBC/hpf and many oxalate crystals. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Ethylene glycol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to toxic metabolites including glycolate, glyoxylate, and oxalate. Patients that ingest ethylene glycol typically present with acute kidney injury, metabolic acidosis, and oxalate crystals on urinalysis.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients that ingest ethylene glycol may present similarly to patients with alcohol intoxication, including central nervous system effects such as disorientation and altered mental status. Ethylene glycol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to toxic metabolites including glycolate, glyoxylate, and oxalate. These metabolites are directly toxic to the kidneys through tubular damage as well as oxalate crystal precipitation causing tubule obstruction, resulting in acute kidney injury. Oxalate crystals are seen on urine microscopy. These metabolites also contribute to an anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Management of ethylene glycol poisoning involves the administration of fomepizole, an alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor that prevents the metabolism of ethylene glycol to its toxic metabolites. For severe ingestions, hemodialysis to remove ethylene glycol and its metabolites from the serum may be necessary.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. Any condition causing organ hypoperfusion and hypoxia can shift metabolism to anaerobic metabolism and increase lactic acid production, leading to lactic acidosis (Choice B). Lactic acidosis itself would be unlikely to cause agitation, confusion, anxiety, and tremulousness. As well, the oxalate crystals on urinalysis are classically caused by ethylene glycol toxicity. Methanol (Choice C) can be metabolized to formic acid, a toxic metabolite that can cause visual disturbances, retinal edema, optic disc hyperemia, gastrointestinal distress, putaminal hemorrhage, and neurologic depression. Paraldehyde (Choice D) has sedative-hypnotic and antiepileptic properties. It is not commonly abused, but poisoning can present with central nervous system depression and metabolic acidosis. It would not cause oxalate crystal precipitation as seen in ethylene glycol toxicity. Sepsis (Choice E) typically presents with fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, and leukocytosis, as well as hypotension and lactic acidosis if the patient is in septic shock, in the setting of a known or suspected infection. This patient has no signs of infection on physical examination. The WBC in his urine is likely caused by inflammation caused by oxalate crystal precipitation, and less likely to be caused by a urinary tract infection.

questions.retrievalTag: 23-Year-Old Anxiety Since Beginning Second Part-Time Courier Photographer
questions.stem: A 23-year-old man comes to the physician because of anxiety since beginning a second part-time job as a courier for a photographer 6 weeks ago. He reports a fear of heights and of riding in elevators. He takes stairs and escalators whenever possible, which prevents him from completing his deliveries on time. He has not had panic attacks. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 118/73 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has an anxious mood and full range of affect. He reports no symptoms of depression or suicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Cognitive behavioral therapy is used to treat specific phobia, which is a marked fear or anxiety that is out of proportion to and consistent in response to a particular object or situation. It classically leads to avoidance behavior and significant distress or impairment in functioning.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: A specific phobia is a fear of an object or situation. It is an anxiety disorder that significantly impairs functioning because it leads to behavior directed at avoiding the object or situation, which can often be excessively time-consuming, as in this patient. Common phobias include heights, animals, enclosed spaces, and needles or injections. According to the DSM-5, diagnostic criteria include marked fear or anxiety that is out of proportion to and consistent in response to the stressor, avoidance behaviors, and significant distress or impairment in functioning. First-line treatment consists of cognitive behavioral therapy with exposure to the patient's specific phobia. This therapy is designed to assist the patient in developing cognitive and behavioral strategies to modify their approach to the phobia. Exposure occurs in repeated and graded amounts, which decreases the fear through extinction. Medication can be used if cognitive behavioral therapy is not sufficient. Medications used include benzodiazepines and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. Interpersonal therapy (Choice B) is used in the treatment of depression and in some eating disorders. It is aimed at helping patients focus on interpersonal problems that contribute to their underlying diagnosis. It is not used in the treatment of specific phobias. α-Adrenergic agonist therapy (Choice C) can be used to treat hypotension (a 1) or hypertension and agitation (a 2). This therapy is not used to treat specific phobias. β-Adrenergic blocker therapy (Choice D) can be used to treat anxiety, tachycardia, and hypertension. While β-adrenergic blocker therapy may reduce the physiologic symptoms provoked by the phobia, it is less effective than cognitive behavioral therapy at treating specific phobias. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor therapy (Choice E) can be used to treat specific phobias but is more commonly used in the treatment of major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder. While used in the treatment of specific phobia, it is usually reserved for cases that are not responsive to cognitive behavioral therapy because there is less evidence for its effectiveness.

questions.retrievalTag: 25-Year-Old Wife Bizarre Behavior Past Months Reports That
questions.stem: A 25-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of bizarre behavior for the past 3 months. She reports that he will only travel to work by taking certain routes and is overly concerned about air quality, routinely inspecting for dust on the furniture and closing the windows when he returns from work. He has organized his office and his home in a particular way and becomes extremely upset when anything is rearranged. He realizes that his demands are unreasonable but says that "I would be very nervous and tense if it were arranged any other way." He has had no changes in sleep pattern. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he reports feeling mildly anxious and depressed "all the time." Results of laboratory studies are within reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive disorder manifests as recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals, which leads to significant dysfunction in social and personal life. First-line pharmacotherapy is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals, which leads to significant dysfunction in social and personal life. It often presents in early adulthood, as in this case. OCD can be distinguished from obsessive-compulsive personality disorder by being ego-dystonic or recognized by the patient as abnormal and undesirable. In contrast, obsessive-compulsive personality disorder is ego-syntonic, meaning that patients do not see their behaviors as problematic. Treatment of OCD includes pharmacotherapy, cognitive behavioral therapy with relaxation techniques, and patient education. First-line pharmacotherapy is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) such as sertraline based on its efficacy in large clinical trials. Clomipramine, a tricyclic antidepressant, and venlafaxine, a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, have also been shown to be efficacious.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and D. Bupropion (Choice A) is a dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, which is used as an antidepressant and smoking cessation aid. This patient's obsessive behaviors and compulsive rituals instead warrant an SSRI. Buspirone (Choice B) is used in the treatment of generalized anxiety disorder. Buspirone acts on the serotonergic neurons in the brain to stimulate serotonin receptors. It is particularly useful as it does not cause addiction, sedation, or tolerance. However, it is not used as first-line therapy in obsessive-compulsive disorder. Clonazepam (Choice C) is a benzodiazepine medication that binds to benzodiazepine sites on the γ-aminobutyric acid (GABAA) receptor, which decreases neuronal activity. Benzodiazepine medications treat symptoms of anxiety and fear by increasing GABA signaling in the amygdala. However, long-term benzodiazepine use is associated with addiction and thus, when possible, nonaddictive medications such as SSRls should be used instead. Haloperidol (Choice D) is used as an antipsychotic medication. Symptoms of psychosis include both positive (delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized thought or speech) and negative (lack of affect, avolition, or apathy) features, which are absent in this case.

questions.retrievalTag: 42-Year-Old Advice Concerning Fear Flying Year Airplane That
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man comes to the physician for advice concerning his fear of flying. One year ago, he was on an airplane that had to abort a takeoff. He was so anxious that he was unable to get back on the plane once the problem was resolved and has been unable to fly since that time. His job requires him to travel, and he is concerned that his fear of flying might jeopardize an anticipated promotion. Twenty years ago, he had a panic attack while giving an oral presentation in a college class. He has mitral valve prolapse. He drinks three cups of coffee daily. His pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a late systolic click followed by a grade 2/6 late systolic murmur. Mental status examination shows a neutral mood and intense affect. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Anxiety disorder due to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. Common phobias include flying, animals, and enclosed spaces. To meet diagnostic criteria, the anxiety symptoms must be persistent (lasting greater than 6 months), and patients must demonstrate distress or social or occupational impairment. The anxiety may center around exposure to or anticipation of the stimulus. Long-term cognitive-behavioral therapy psychotherapy is the most effective treatment, though short-term benzodiazepines may be utilized in certain patients whose feared situations occur infrequently.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Anxiety disorder due to a general medical condition (Choice A) and substance-induced anxiety disorder (Choice E) typically manifest as generalized anxiety or panic symptoms rather than specific phobia. Further, this patient does not demonstrate other physical symptoms or vital sign abnormalities consistent with a medical condition or intoxication. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice B) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment. This patient's remote performance anxiety, a common anxiety, is no longer clinically relevant. The patient's anxiety symptoms are currently specific to flying. Panic disorder (Choice C) is a relatively common psychiatric disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. Agoraphobia, or the fear of leaving one's home, frequently accompanies panic disorder. This patient with anxiety does not endorse associated physical symptoms or a fear of leaving his home generally.

questions.retrievalTag: 14-Year-Old Well-Child Examination Mother Says Self-Centered Shows Decreasing
questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. His mother says her son is self-centered and shows decreasing interest in his family. He showers twice daily but will not keep his room neat. He frequently shifts from being pleasant and cooperative to being very disagreeable. He has not begun dating. Pubic hair development began at the age of 12 years. Examination shows no facial hair and minimal axillary hair. Pubic hair and genital development are Tanner stage 3. Which of the following is the most accurate assessment of this patient's development?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Advanced; Cognitive/Social Development: advanced

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Advanced; Cognitive/Social Development: delayed

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Advanced; Cognitive/Social Development: normal

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Delayed; Cognitive/Social Development: advanced

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Delayed; Cognitive/Social Development: delayed

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Delayed; Cognitive/Social Development: normal

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Normal; Cognitive/Social Development: advanced

answerChoices.label: H
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Normal; Cognitive/Social Development: delayed

answerChoices.label: I
answerChoices.text: Physical/Sexual Development: Normal; Cognitive/Social Development: normal

questions.educationalObjective: Normal adolescence is characterized by an array of physical, sexual, cognitive, and social changes. Distinguishing normal patterns from pathologic changes is important for identifying patients who may benefit from treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: normal advanced delayed normal This patient's presentation is consistent with normal adolescent development. Boys typically enter puberty between the ages of 9 and 12. Puberty is associated with a variety of physical and psychological changes associated with increased sex hormone production driven by follicle-stimulating hormone, including development of secondary sexual characteristics (eg, pubic and axillary hair), increased stature, increased bone growth, and changes in body fat composition and lean muscle mass. Mood instability and psychological stress associated with these changes is common.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and H. Choices A, B, and C suggest advanced physical and sexual development with variable degrees of cognitive and social development, which may be seen in disorders associated with precocious puberty. Causes of precocious puberty in adolescents include central nervous system lesions, such as pituitary adenomas, and peripheral disorders including congenital adrenal hyperplasia and testicular or ovarian tumors. This boy's display of Tanner stage 3 characteristics and lack of facial and axillary hair is typical for his age. Choices D, E, and F suggest delayed physical and sexual development with variable degrees of cognitive and social development. Delayed puberty should be considered in males who lack secondary sexual characteristics after the age of 14 and in females after the age of 13. This may occur in the setting of an inadequate central production of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (eg, constitutional delay of growth and puberty, or CDGP) or in the setting of hypogonadism. Most adolescents with CDGP display normal cognitive and social development, although patients with hypogonadism associated with gonadal dysgenesis 45,X (Turner syndrome) or seminiferous tubule dysgenesis (Klinefelter syndrome) may display a delay in these milestones. Choices G and H suggest normal physical and sexual development with either advanced or delayed cognitive and social development. Puberty and adolescence are associated with a variety of changes including increased mood variability, reduced behavioral inhibition, and increased risk-taking behavior. Stress, a changing sense of identity and autonomy, and a developing social identity may lead to increased conflict between adolescents and their families.

extractionWarnings: Answer choices appear as a two-column matrix in the source; preserved as labeled combined physical/sexual and cognitive/social development options.

questions.retrievalTag: 27-Year-Old Woman Gravida Para Husband Progressive Irritability Anxiety
questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician with her husband because of progressive irritability and anxiety since the birth of her child 3 weeks ago. Pregnancy was complicated by premature labor that was managed with bed rest. Delivery occurred at term with no complications. Examination of her newborn at birth showed no abnormalities. The patient reports that he feeds well and appears healthy, but she has constant worries about his health and her own health. She adds that she has had a decreased appetite and difficulty sleeping. Her husband notes that she has been increasingly withdrawn from family and friends and that she has reported thoughts of suicide. The patient has no history of serious illness or major depressive disorder. Her only medication is a multivitamin. She appears tired. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has an irritable mood and depressed affect. She is oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: A diagnosis of major depressive disorder is made when a patient experiences five or more of the following symptoms over a 2-week period or greater: depressed mood, anhedonia, change in appetite or weight, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation or retardation, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness, diminished ability to think or concentrate, and recurrent thoughts of death including suicidal ideation with or without a plan. Postpartum unipolar major depression is a type of major depressive disorder that typically occurs prior to or shortly after giving birth.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: A diagnosis of major depressive disorder is made when a patient experiences five or more of the following symptoms over a 2-week period or longer: depressed mood, anhedonia, change in appetite or weight, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation or retardation, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness, diminished ability to think or concentrate, and recurrent thoughts of death including suicidal ideation with or without a plan. This patient demonstrates several of these symptoms since the birth of her baby. Major depressive disorder with a postpartum onset can have many adverse consequences including a decreased likelihood of breastfeeding, impaired bonding with the infant, marital discord, poorer infant health, infanticide, and suicide. Antidepressants such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are first-line medications for major depressive disorder and work best when combined with psychotherapy such as cognitive-behavioral therapy. Hospitalization is often required for stabilization in acute cases of postpartum depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) is characterized by anxiety, depressed mood, or inappropriate behavior in response to a profound life change. Adjustment disorder is diagnosed when the patient does not meet criteria for another psychiatric disorder. In this case, the patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder instead, so while her depression may be influenced by her having recently given birth, it does not qualify as adjustment disorder. Bipolar disorder (Choice B) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can sometimes be accompanied by psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. This patient does not demonstrate mania currently and has no history of past manic episodes, so unipolar depression is more likely. Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice C) requires the presence of excessive worry about numerous aspects of daily life occurring on more days than not for at least 6 months, with a consequent inability to control symptoms of anxiety, and with the presence of at least three other symptoms (irritability, muscle tension, sleep problems, poor concentration, fatigue, or restlessness). These symptoms are absent in this patient. Post-traumatic stress disorder (Choice E) presents with symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, dissociation, avoidance, and/or depressed mood following a life-threatening traumatic event, which persist for over 1 month. This patient does not meet these criteria.

questions.retrievalTag: 32-Year-Old Woman Depressed Mood Weeks Went Drinking Binge
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of depressed mood for 2 weeks; she went on a drinking binge over the weekend and has been binge eating for 2 days but has not purged. She has a history of bulimia nervosa. She has been sexually active with multiple partners over the past month; she uses an oral contraceptive, and her partners use condoms. She takes paroxetine for dysthymic disorder. Abdominal examination shows mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant. She has poor eye contact and appears close to tears. She describes many new stressors in her life and has thought about taking a full bottle of aspirin. Which of the following is the most appropriate question to ask this patient?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: "Do you exercise regularly to relieve stress?"

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: "How do you feel about attending an alcohol treatment program?"

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: "How would you feel about entering the hospital?"

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: "Is there someone I could call to get some support for you?"

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: "What would you like me to do?"

questions.educationalObjective: When a patient expresses suicidality and possesses several acute suicide risk factors (eg, a planned method, recent suicide preparations, acute mood changes, increased impulsivity, or command hallucinations), hospitalization should be considered. The physician should ask the patient how they feel about being hospitalized, which determines whether the physician will pursue a voluntary or involuntary hospitalization.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient possesses several acute risk factors for suicide, indicating that psychiatric hospitalization should be considered. Her acute suicide risk factors include suicidal ideation with a planned method, acutely depressed mood, increased impulsivity, and increased substance use. Other acute risk factors include a strong urge to follow through with the suicide plan, suicide preparations (eg, writing a suicide note), acute anxiety, feeling like a burden, social isolation, and command auditory hallucinations. The physician should also inquire about the patient's access to lethal means (eg, gun or medication stockpiles) to assist in risk stratification. When patients possess several acute risk factors (especially suicidal ideation with a realistic plan, a strong desire to die, or suicide preparations), hospitalization should be strongly considered to keep the patient safe and expeditiously treat the underlying psychiatric disorder. The physician should assess the patient's willingness to be hospitarnzed and pursue voluntary hospitalization if the patient is willing. If the patient declines hospitalization, the physician may attempt to make a safety plan with the patient for outpatient management or pursue an involuntary hospitalization. Though acute risk factors are the most important to consider when deciding whether to hospitalize a patient, chronic risk factors (eg, male gender, minority status, chronic medical or psychiatric disorders, or a history of suicide attempts) further increase a patient's suicide risk.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Asking the patient if she exercises regularly (Choice A) would miss an opportunity to inquire further about her suicidal ideation. This patient's suicidal ideation, a serious safety issue, should be urgently evaluated. Suggesting an alcohol treatment program (Choice B) or increasing a patient's social supports (Choice D) in the outpatient setting may be appropriate if a patient expressing suicidal ideation does not demonstrate a suicide risk that is significantly increased compared to baseline and is able to make a safety plan. However, this patient's acute suicide risk is significantly increased, so hospitalization should be considered. Asking the patient what the physician should do (Choice E) is not a specific enough question. The physician should instead directly ask how the patient feels about hospitalization, which will inform the physician's management.

questions.retrievalTag: 37-Year-Old Woman Husband 1-Year History Fatigue Intermittently Blurred
questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of a 1-year history of fatigue, intermittently blurred vision, and numbness of her legs that is exacerbated by walking long distances. Her husband adds that she has begun to have difficulty remembering where she put things around the house and says that when he points out her memory problem to her, she does not seem to care. She states that she does the best she can but does not have the energy to devote to his concerns. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Sensation to pinprick is decreased over the lower extremities. Muscle strength in the upper and lower extremities is 3/5. On mental status examination, her mood and affect are incongruously bright. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She recalls two of three objects after 5 minutes. Which of the following abnormalities is most likely to be present in this patient on imaging studies?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Edema of the right cerebral hemisphere

questions.educationalObjective: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. Fatigue and exercise-induced symptom exacerbation are common. Treatment includes corticosteroids for symptomatic management of acute flares and long-term disease-modifying treatments such as interferon or monoclonal antibodies, which decrease the frequency of future episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Multiple sclerosis, the most prevalent immune-mediated demyelinating disease, can present initially with lesions in several central nervous system (CNS) locations. According to the McDonald criteria for multiple sclerosis diagnosis, patients must have evidence of demyelinating lesions that are separated in time and location. This evidence can be based on subjective symptoms or imaging evidence. This patient demonstrated several episodes of optic neuritis separated in time, along with dysfunction of the dorsal column- medial lemniscus tracts (decreased sensation to pinprick), corticospinal tracts (weakness), frontal lobes (incongruently bright affect), and medial temporal lobe (decreased spatial and recall memory), representing separation in space. Fatigue and symptom exacerbation with exercise are also common in multiple sclerosis. MRI of the brain typically discloses focal white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular region, cortex, subcortical region, cerebellum, and/or spine. Oligoclonal bands of immunoglobulins, indicating immune overactivation, can be found in the cerebrospinal fluid but are not necessary for diagnosis. Treatment includes corticosteroids for symptomatic management of acute flares and long-term disease-modifying treatments such as interferon or monoclonal antibodies, which decrease the frequency of future episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) (Choice A) refers to an abnormal tangle of blood vessels resulting in communication between an artery and vein. AVMs can be located throughout the CNS although they are typically solitary. Patients with multiple AVMs typically demonstrate additional physical examination signs of heritable syndromes (eg, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia). Edema of the right cerebral hemisphere (Choice B) typically leads to left-sided motor and sensory deficits. This patient demonstrates bilateral motor and sensory deficits. Enlarged cortical sulci (Choice C) may be demonstrated in patients with dementia secondary to a neurodegenerative disease. This patient illustrates focal neurologic deficits (eg, decreased sensation or motor weakness), which would be atypical of dementia secondary to a neurodegenerative process. Left cerebellar atrophy (Choice E) leads to ipsilateral motor incoordination. Optic neuritis, numbness, and incongruent affect would be atypical.

questions.retrievalTag: 18-Year-Old Emergency Department Friends Hour After They Found
questions.stem: An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by friends 1 hour after they found him on the couch at a party unable to move. Earlier that evening, he had been using synthetic heroin. On arrival, his pulse is 85/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Examination shows stiffness in all extremities, drooling, and slow response to questions. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?
questions.educationalObjective: Synthetic opioids have been found to be contaminated with MPTP, a neurotoxin associated with acute parkinsonism. Parkinsonism manifests with muscle rigidity, bradykinesia, resting tremor, and postural instability.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Synthetic opioids have been found to be contaminated with MPTP, a neurotoxin associated with acute, irreversible parkinsonism. The toxic metabolite of MPTP has a high affinity for dopamine transporters. This metabolite is transported to dopaminergic nerve terminals in the substantia nigra, leading to neuronal damage. Parkinsonism manifests with muscle rigidity, bradykinesia, resting tremor, and postural instability. Drooling secondary to bradykinesia and poor motor control of the tongue may occur. Some patients with MPTP- associated parkinsonism have been treated with carbidopα-levodopa, which may improve symptoms but does not cure the disease.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, and E. The cerebellar vermis (Choice A) controls the motor function of the axial and proximal limb muscles and mediates body posture and balance. Lesions of the cerebellar vermis lead to severe gait ataxia (from truncal instability), titubation (bobbing of the head or axial body), and nystagmus. Parkinsonism would be atypical. The globus pallidus (Choice B) is a basal ganglia structure containing inhibitory neurons that coordinate with the subthalamic nucleus to control goal-oriented movement. Lesions may lead to dystonia and tremor. However, the toxic metabolite of MPTP instead has a high affinity for dopamine transporters in the substantia nigra. The locus ceruleus (Choice C) contains cholinergic neurons that project widely throughout the central nervous system and mediate attention and memory. Alzheimer dementia results from decreased cholinergic signaling from the locus ceruleus. Parkinsonism would be atypical. The mammillary bodies (Choice D) are limbic system structures that assist in recall memory. Damage to the mammillary bodies is associated with Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Parkinsonism would be atypical. The nucleus accumbens (Choice E) integrates information from the limbic system with frontal brain areas, playing a role in reward, motivation, and emotion. Parkinsonism would be atypical.

questions.retrievalTag: 47-Year-Old Wife Unbearable Behavior During Past Weeks Wife
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of "unbearable" behavior during the past 2 weeks. His wife reports that he has been talking about his various inventions constantly and recently bought a new recreational vehicle, even though they do not travel long distances. He repeatedly tells his wife that "the time to make and enjoy money has come." He has been openly critical of their pastor's prayers during church services. He claims that the pastor does not know what he is talking about because "God is talking to me, not him." His wife says that he has had episodes of similar behavior during the past 10 years that have been more tolerable. He has peptic ulcer disease treated with ranitidine and hypertension treated with triamterene. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 150/95 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is relaxed and talkative, jumping from one topic to another. He claims that his wife does not understand him and that she has been plotting with the pastor against him. He denies any problems and says that he feels great and is capable of great things. Laboratory findings are within the reference range. Urine toxicology screening is positive for marijuana. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of hypomania or mania and may include episodes of depression. Acute mania typically manifests with euphoric or irritable mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Psychotic features, such as paranoid delusions, may also occur.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of mania. Acute mania typically manifests with euphoric or irritable mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking (as evidenced by this patient's pressured speech with a flight of ideas), irresponsibility (as evidenced by this patient's out-of-character spending), and grandiose thinking (as evidenced by this patient believing that God is talking to him). Some patients demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, paranoid delusions or hallucinations). Type 1 bipolar disorder is this patient's most likely diagnosis. Though patients with type 1 bipolar disorder need to demonstrate a history of mania to meet diagnostic criteria, patients with type 2 bipolar disorder must demonstrate major depressive episodes in addition to hypomanic episodes (less severe and shorter episodes of mania without psychotic features). Management requires mood stabilizing therapy (eg, mood stabilizers or mood stabilizing antipsychotics) for acute mania. Once acute mood episodes resolve, patients with bipolar disorder should continue mood stabilizing maintenance therapy to prevent future episodes of mania or depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: B, C, D, and E. Delusional disorder (Choice B) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. Mania symptoms are atypical of delusional disorder. Mood disorder due to a general medical condition (Choice C) refers to acute mania related to medical conditions such as hyperthyroidism, Cushing disease, traumatic brain injury, seizure, stroke, brain neoplasm, meningitis, or neurosyphilis. This patient does not demonstrate physical examination signs of endocrine or neurologic etiologies of mania. His mild hypertension in the absence of other physical examination findings is likely essential hypertension (given his increased BMI), which is not associated with mania. Schizophrenia (Choice D) is characterized by a chronic course of delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, or alogia) outside of manic or depressive episodes. This patient's paranoid delusion is secondary to acute mania. This patient's episodic course without evidence of residual symptoms between episodes is also consistent with a mood disorder rather than a psychotic disorder. Substance-induced mood disorder (Choice E) refers to acute mania secondary to the use of a substance (most commonly stimulants and hallucinogens such as marijuana). Though this patient's urine toxicology was positive for marijuana, the patient did not demonstrate evidence of marijuana use prior to his previous manic episodes, indicating that bipolar disorder is more likely.

questions.retrievalTag: 47-Year-Old Woman Hours After Onset Heart Palpitations Myocardial
questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician 2 hours after the onset of heart palpitations. She had a myocardial infarction 6 years ago and is suddenly afraid that she might be having another heart attack. She does not have chest pain. She says that 2 hours ago, she was feeling "extremely good and enjoying how things seemed to be moving slowly around me." An exercise stress test 2 months ago showed no abnormalities. She has been taking alprazolam up to three times daily for 3 years for anxiety and took her last dose 3 hours ago. Her father died of a myocardial infarction at the age of 46 years. She had not used an illicit substance for 20 years, but 2 months ago, she was promoted to a managerial position and began smoking marijuana daily to relax. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 150/95 mm Hg. Physical examination shows dry oral mucosa and injected conjunctivae. Neurologic examination shows difficulty with finger-nose testing. On mental status examination, she has an anxious affect, and she repeatedly asks for something to be done. Her speech is staccato and rushed but clear and rational. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current symptoms?

questions.retrievalTag: 42-Year-Old Emergency Department Police After They Found Hiding
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after they found him hiding under a parked truck. Police say the patient claimed to be hiding from "dementors" who would look him in the eyes and make him an alien through "viral-spatial worms." During the past 5 years, he has been brought to the emergency department eight times for similar behavior. He has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not use illicit drugs. He lives alone and does not have friends. He appears disheveled. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is fully oriented. He makes poor eye contact and speaks quickly and loudly. He has an anxious affect. His thought process is predominantly linear. During the examination, he opens his eyes only after he counts out loud to 13 and says that doing so helps him to counteract "evil forces." Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Schizophrenia is characterized by at least 6 months exhibiting two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, or alogia).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, or alogia). Negative symptoms may also manifest as poor personal hygiene. Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and decreased functioning. The total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months to meet diagnostic criteria, which is suggested in this case by the patient's similar episodes over the past 5 years. The cause is thought to include dopamine dysregulation, and brain imaging typically shows decreased cortical volume and enlarged ventricles. The treatment of schizophrenia is with antipsychotics, which can be divided into two groups: typical (haloperidol, fluphenazine, and chlorpromazine) and atypical (risperidone, quetiapine, and olanzapine).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and D. Bipolar disorder (Choice A) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can sometimes be accompanied by psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. This patient is demonstrating psychotic symptoms but not mood symptoms, which indicates schizophrenia as a more accurate diagnosis. Delusional disorder (Choice B) features one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. This patient appears to also be experiencing hallucinations, which are not typical of delusional disorder. Obsessive-compulsive disorder (Choice C) manifests as recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts that the patient tries to relieve or neutralize by repetitive rituals, which leads to significant dysfunction in social and personal life. Delusions and hallucinations are not present in obsessive-compulsive disorder. Schizoid personality disorder (Choice D), a cluster A personality disorder, manifests as extreme social detachment and a cold, restricted affect. These patients typically disengage from society. However, acute delusions and hallucinations, as seen in this case, are absent.

questions.retrievalTag: Previously Healthy 37-Year-Old 6-Month History Depressed Mood Fatigue
questions.stem: A previously healthy 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of depressed mood, fatigue, poor concentration, and difficulty sleeping. He works as an accountant and has had difficulty staying awake at work, particularly in the afternoon. He says that his wife sleeps in a separate room because of his loud snoring. He drinks alcohol at social functions only. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 109 kg (240 lb); BMI is 35 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a mildly depressed mood.

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Obstructive sleep apnea features repetitive obstruction of the pharyngeal airway during sleep and typically occurs in patients with obesity. Patients commonly experience hypoxemia several times nightly with fragmented sleep leading to daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and poor concentration. Because of disruption of sleep architecture and nocturnal hypoxemia, it may cause depression. F'

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) features repetitive obstruction of the pharyngeal airway during sleep and typically occurs in patients with obesity because of a combination of structural, vascular, and neuromuscular factors. Though older males are most commonly affected, younger and female patients may also be affected. Patients commonly experience hypoxemia several times nightly with fragmented sleep leading to daytime sleepiness, fatigue, poor concentration, and irritability. Snoring, related to airway obstruction, is commonly associated with OSA. Because of overlapping symptoms, OSA may be confused with depression. Alternatively, because of abnormal sleep architecture or intermittent nocturnal hypoxemia, OSA may cause depression. In patients with depression secondary to OSA, treatment should include continuous positive airway pressure, which addresses OSA and consequently treats the associated depression symptoms. Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, F, and H. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with depression or anxiety symptoms. This patient has no history of a recent stressor. Bereavement (Choice B), or uncomplicated grief reaction, refers to a person's emotional response after a loved one dies and can include sadness, shock, anguish, guilt, regret, difficulty concentrating, decreased interest in usual activities, preoccupation with thoughts and memories of the deceased, and neurovegetative symptoms such as sleep, appetite, and energy disturbances. This patient did not experience the death of a loved one. Bipolar disorder, depressed (Choice C) refers to a depressive episode in patients with underlying bipolar disorder, which implies a history of manic or hypomanic episodes. Cyclothymic disorder (Choice D) refers to alternating mania and depression symptoms that do not meet full criteria for major depressive or manic episodes. This patient has no history of mania, and his depression symptoms are related to a general medical condition rather than a primary psychiatric cause. Dysthymic disorder (Choice E), more commonly known as persistent depressive disorder, refers to depression symptoms lasting longer than 2 years. This patient's depression symptoms are related to a general medication condition rather than a primary psychiatric cause and have not endured for 2 years. Major depressive disorder (Choice F) includes 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, and appetite disturbance). questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Patients with adjustment disorder may experience associated depression or anxiety symptoms but by definition do not meet diagnostic criteria of depression or anxiety disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in school, work, or social functioning. Adjustment disorders are typically associated with anxiety, depression, or behavioral symptoms without meeting the criteria of anxiety or depressive disorders. For example, this patient has depressed mood, decreased appetite, and potential anhedonia (as evidenced by isolation) but not guilt, decreased energy, decreased sleep, or suicidal ideation. Further, she does not meet the duration requirement for a major depressive disorder diagnosis (2 weeks). questions.retrievalTag: 32-Year-Old Financial Analyst Employer'S Request Bizarre Behavior Poor
questions.stem: A 32-year-old financial analyst comes to the physician at her employer's request because of bizarre behavior and poor work productivity over the past 6 months. She has been arriving late, leaving early, and frequently taking long breaks. She has had daily mood swings; sometimes she appears withdrawn, lethargic, and belligerent and other times she appears energetic and loquacious. Over the past 2 weeks, she has had several arguments with coworkers in which she accused them of stealing or sabotaging her work. This morning, she threatened to physically assault her supervisor after he questioned her about a 2-hour absence from work. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. The pupils are dilated and reactive to light. Deep tendon reflexes are increased. On mental status examination, she is angry but cooperative. She describes her supervisor's unfairness and the unfriendliness of other analysts; she suspects that they are jealous of her. She speaks in a loud, rapid voice, frequently interrupting the physician. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She is able to recall three of three objects after 5 minutes and quickly and accurately performs 2-digit calculations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine is an addictive recreational drug that typically causes euphoria, restlessness, and increased sympathetic tone. Withdrawal from cocaine can cause a depressed mood, fatigue, and slowing of activity. F'

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Cocaine blocks the presynaptic reuptake of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, which increases the concentration of these substances in the synaptic cleft. Cocaine is an addictive recreational drug that typically causes euphoria, restlessness, and increased sympathetic tone leading to tachycardia, hypertension, hyperreflexia, and pupillary dilation. Increased synaptic serotonin leads to euphoria, while increased synaptic dopamine leads to addiction (and in severe cases, hallucinations and paranoia). Withdrawal from cocaine can cause depressed mood, fatigue, and slowing of activity. The patient's daily mood swings are likely caused by cocaine intoxication and withdrawal, and chronic cocaine use can lead to paranoia. Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, E, F, G, and H. Periods of lethargy and daily mood swings are not characteristic of alcohol withdrawal.
