body: The chronic use of dopamine-2 (D2) antagonists (particularly hi,gh potency D2 antagonists such as risperidone) may upregulate D2 receptors in the basal ganglia, leading to dopamine receptor hypersensitivity and consequent tardive dyskinesia. Tardive dyskinesia is a syndrome of involuntary movements (eg, lip smacking, choreoathetoid movements of the tongue). When D2 antagonists are discontinued, dopaminergic signaling increases and transiently worsens tardive dyskinesia. Since tardive dyskinesia is not fully reversible for some patients, patients on antipsychotic medication or metoclopramide should be closely monitored for the development of tardive dyskinesia (or alternative agents should be selected). Management involves reducing the dose of the offending medication and/or switching to a medication that does not cause potent D2 antagonism. Vesicular monoamine transporter-2 inhibitors (eg, tetrabenazine) can also improve tardive dyskinesia by depleting dopamine stores.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Atrophy of the caudate nucleus (Choice A) is common in Huntington disease, which typically presents with progressive choreoathetoid movements and dementia. However, this patient's choreoathetoid movements that were exacerbated by antipsychotic discontinuation are more consistent with chronic antipsychotic medication use. Increased acetylcholine (Choice B) may modulate dopamine signaling in the basal ganglia. Cholinergic dysregulation contributes to parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability). Choreoathetoid movements are more consistent with tardive dyskinesia. Loss of volume in the basal ganglia (Choice D) is common in Parkinson disease. Parkinson disease is characterized by bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability. Vascular changes in the small vessels (Choice E) underlie vascular dementia. Vascular dementia typically presents with stepwise declines in cognition along with focal neurologic deficits. This patient does not clearly demonstrate dementia or neurologic deficits that can be localized to one region of the central nervous system.

questions.retrievalTag: Five Weeks After Being Discharged From Hospital After
questions.stem: Five weeks after being discharged from the hospital after treatment for a psychotic episode, a 27-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow- up examination. During hospitalization, he claimed that he was instructed by the Lord to found a new religion and that a famous gospel singer was in love with him. Today, he says that he still hears the voices of the Lord and members of the church he attends in his apartment and when he shops in the supermarket. He no longer believes the world needs a new religion because the Lord is no longer instructing him to create one. He states, "My father in heaven tells me that he is at peace, and therefore, so am I." Current medications include risperidone and lorazepam. He drinks one to two beers on weekends. He used marijuana regularly in college but has abstained for the past 5 years. He appears clean and is casually dressed. His temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/72 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a calm affect. He is cooperative, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time. Based on this information, which of the following is the most likely current diagnosis for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Patients with schizoaffective disorder demonstrate psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms) along with significant mood episodes (eg, major depressive episodes, manic episodes). Unlike patients with bipolar disorder, patients with schizoaffective disorder experience psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia spectrum disorders (eg, schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder) demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, or alogia). To meet diagnostic criteria, patients must demonstrate functional deficits, and the total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months. Patients with schizoaffective disorder must demonstrate mood episodes (eg, major depressive episodes or manic episodes) for a substantial portion of the duration of the illness. Manic episodes typically include a euphoric or irritable mood, a decreased need for sleep, increased energy, grandiose thinking (as in this patient), and increased goal- directed activity. By definition, patients with schizoaffective disorder also experience psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations) in the absence of mood symptoms for at least 2 weeks, as in this patient. Management requires antipsychotic medication and may also include adjunctive antidepressant medication (if depressive episodes are predominant) or mood stabilizing medication (if manic episodes are predominant).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Bipolar disorder (Choice A) is a mood disorder characterized by episodic manic and depressive symptoms. Cyclothymic disorder (Choice B) refers to alternating manic and depressive symptoms that do not meet the full criteria for major depressive or manic episodes. Manic episodes may include psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations), which may be conceptualized as being secondary to mania. This patient shows psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms. Delusional disorder (Choice C) is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions for a month or longer without other schizophrenia symptoms. Hallucinations and mania symptoms are atypical of delusional disorder. Substance-induced mood disorder (Choice E) is unlikely given the patient's mild alcohol use and remote history of cannabis use. Though ongoing cannabis use could explain consistent hallucinations, cannabis use years prior to this patient's presentation would not likely explain his current psychotic symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Unconscious -Year-Old Emergency Department Parents Family Emigrated From
questions.stem: An unconscious 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents. The family emigrated from rural Southeast Asia 4 years ago. An interpreter is used because the parents do not speak English. They say, "Our child has been possessed by a spirit since birth who speaks through his mouth with spit and blood and then takes his powers away." He has had these spells all of his life. He does not attend school. They recently sacrificed a piglet as an offering to the spirit, but there has been no change in the child's condition. The parents seem devoted. He awakens during the physical examination and smiles vacantly at his parents. He appears confused and speaks in monosyllables. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Generalized seizures present with sudden onset syncope with characteristic movement depending on the type of generalized seizure (eg, tonic-clonic). Typically, the patient presents in a postictal state with delayed recovery of mental status. The definitive diagnosis of seizures is completed with EEG.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Generalized tonic-clonic seizures are characterized by a sudden loss of consciousness followed by a stiffening of all of the muscles of the arms, legs, chest, and back (tonic phase), progressing to jerking or twitching of all of the muscles (clonic phase). After the seizure, postictal confusion is common. Although the parents' description of the episodes is limited, the spells that the parents describe are likely seizures, and a postictal state is likely the cause of this patient's vacant expression and confusion after he awakens. The definitive diagnosis of seizures and epilepsy is completed through EEG. Treatment is with anti-epileptic medication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, E, and F. Contacting child protective services (Choice A) or family therapy (Choice C) would not be the appropriate next step. The parents seem devoted to the child and although they have a different understanding of the patient's condition, there are no signs of child abuse. A psychoeducational assessment (Choice B) is typically done to evaluate a child's learning and development, and can help diagnose a learning disability, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, or developmental disability. This patient's confusion is less likely to be caused by a developmental disability and is more likely to be caused by a postictal state. A throat culture (Choice D) and x-ray of the chest (Choice E) can be helpful in diagnosing bacterial infections of the upper respiratory tract or lower respiratory tract, respectively. While infections can predispose patients with epilepsy to increasing seizure frequency or breakthrough seizures, this patient has no focal infectious symptoms, and the diagnosis of seizures must be made first. An ECG (Choice F) can be helpful in diagnosing causes of cardiogenic syncope, including hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, which classically demonstrates left ventricular hypertrophy and non-specific T wave abnormalities. An ECG can also help to diagnose conditions such as long QT syndrome or Brugada syndrome, which are other potential causes of cardiac syncope. Syncope can be differentiated from seizures by the lack of a postictal state.

questions.retrievalTag: 30-Year-Old Woman Frontal Headaches Stomach Upset Poor Appetite
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman has had frontal headaches, stomach upset, and poor appetite for 4 weeks; she has had a 2-kg (5-lb) weight loss during this period. She has pain in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen 1 to 3 hours after eating dinner. Over-the-counter antacids have been ineffective. She blames herself for not taking better care of her body. The headaches occur at night and awaken her. She feels fatigued in the morning and has difficulty getting up and going to work. Over the past month, her supervisor has complained about her recent errors, which she attributes to difficulty concentrating. She says that she is so tired when she comes home that she has stopped going out with her friends and just goes to sleep. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). In many patients, physical symptoms are predominant, and depressive symptoms are interpreted as arising from a physical problem (in part secondary to the stigma of mental illness).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia (eg, decreased interest in socializing), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. This patient has poor appetite, guilt, low energy, difficulty concentrating, and anhedonia. Though some patients are able to express a depressed mood, others (particularly women, elderly patients, and African American patients) may present with primarily somatic symptoms. Depression and somatic symptoms (especially pain symptoms) commonly co-occur, which is postulated to arise from a common descending pain pathway. Patients may interpret their depression symptoms as being related to physical disease to normalize the symptoms, which is related to the stigma of mental illness. Treatment of MDD should include psychotherapy and antidepressant medication. Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, E, and F. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Patients with adjustment disorder may experience associated depression or anxiety symptoms but by definition do not meet the diagnostic criteria of depression or anxiety disorders. Borderline personality disorder (Choice B) is a cluster B personality disorder that features an unstable sense of self, emotional dysregulation, and tumultuous relationships. To unconsciously fulfill emotional needs, patients with borderline personality disorder make suicidal estures, avoid abandonment, and either idealize or devalue other eo le. None of these s cholo ical defenses are evident in This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia (eg, decreased interest in socializing), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms disrupt everyday functioning. This patient has poor appetite, guilt, low energy, difficulty concentrating, and anhedonia. Though some patients are able to express a depressed mood, others (particularly women, elderly patients, and African American patients) may present with primarily somatic symptoms. Depression and somatic symptoms (especially pain symptoms) commonly co-occur, which is postulated to arise from a common descending pain pathway. Patients may interpret their depression symptoms as being related to physical disease to normalize the symptoms, which is related to the stigma of mental illness. Treatment of MDD should include psychotherapy and antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, E, and F. Adjustment disorder (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Patients with adjustment disorder may experience associated depression or anxiety symptoms but by definition do not meet the diagnostic criteria of depression or anxiety disorders. Borderline personality disorder (Choice B) is a cluster B personality disorder that features an unstable sense of self, emotional dysregulation, and tumultuous relationships. To unconsciously fulfill emotional needs, patients with borderline personality disorder make suicidal gestures, avoid abandonment, and either idealize or devalue other people. None of these psychological defenses are evident in this patient. Dysthymic disorder (Choice C), more commonly known as persistent depressive disorder, refers to depressive symptoms lasting longer than 2 years. Patients with dysthymic disorder do not meet major depressive disorder criteria. In somatization disorder (Choice E), more commonly known as somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. Patients persistently devote excessive time and energy to these symptoms or related health concerns (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor). This patient does not demonstrate preoccupation or maladaptive care-seeking behaviors related to her symptoms. Ulcerative colitis (Choice F) typically manifests as chronic diarrhea that is commonly bloody, along with fever, fatigue, and abdominal pain. This patient does not endorse diarrhea and endorses other symptoms such as frontal headaches, which are inconsistent with ulcerative colitis.

questions.retrievalTag: 42-Year-Old 6-Month History Sexual Problems Achieve Maintain Erections
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of sexual problems. He can achieve and maintain erections but is rarely able to ejaculate during intercourse. He has erections on awakening in the morning and nocturnal emissions approximately once monthly. He has a 2-year history of coronary artery disease well controlled with metoprolol, lisinopril, and aspirin. He and his wife of 12 years divorced 3 years ago. Over the past 6 months, he has been sexually active with two female partners, and they use condoms for contraception. Examination shows a normal-appearing penis and testes. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's sexual dysfunction?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Disruption of arterial inflow to the penis by metoprolol

questions.educationalObjective: Psychological factors such as performance anxiety or marital conflict are relatively common causes of sexual dysfunction. Patients with sexual dysfunction secondary to performance anxiety typically continue to experience nocturnal tumescence and emissions and lack anatomical abnormalities of the genitalia on physical examination.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Psychological factors such as performance anxiety or marital conflict can exert a strong influence on sexual function in both men and women. In men, anxiety can lead to decreased libido, decreased ability to obtain or maintain an erection, premature ejaculation, or decreased ability to ejaculate. New sexual partners are a risk factor for performance anxiety. Patients with sexual dysfunction secondary to performance anxiety typically continue to experience nocturnal tumescence and emissions and lack anatomical abnormalities of the genitalia on physical examination. Treatment strategies include cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness meditation training, or pharmacotherapy (eg, serotonergic anxiolytic medications or phosphodiesterase inhibitors).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and E. Decreased pituitary production of gonadotropins (Choice A), specifically follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, typically leads to decreased testosterone production and consequent decreased libido rather than orgasmic dysfunction. Patients typically demonstrate decreased testicular size. Metoprolol (Choice B) is a β1-selective β-adrenergic blocker, acting specifically on cardiac tissue. Metoprolol would not lead to disruption of arterial inflow to the penis which may be seen with α-adrenergic agonism or β2 blockade. Obstruction at the bladder neck (Choice C) commonly occurs in benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Though BPH and sexual dysfunction can co-occur in older male patients with cardiovascular risk factors, this relatively young patient is at a decreased risk for developing BPH and does not demonstrate urinary symptoms consistent with BPH. Testicular insensitivity to testosterone (Choice E), as in androgen insensitivity syndrome, is associated with decreased libido. However, this would present with decreased sperm production and potentially with phenotypic changes such as loss of testicular volume.

questions.retrievalTag: 16-Year-Old Girl Father Unusual Behavior Since Returning From
questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father because of unusual behavior since returning from a party early this morning. Her father reports that when she arrived home, she barricaded herself in her room and refused to respond to his pleas to open the door. When he entered the room 1 hour later, he found her huddled in a corner in the dark, saying that the police wanted to kill her. She has no history of medical or psychiatric illness. Her pulse is 115/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. The pupils are 2 mm, equal, and reactive to light and accommodation. The conjunctivae are injected. On mental status examination, she appears apprehensive but is cooperative. Her speech is slow with a prolonged response latency. She believes that she is being followed by plainclothes police officers who want to assassinate her. She hears a male voice making threatening comments. She is oriented to person and place but not to time. She can perform double-digit money calculations but not serial sevens. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Marijuana is a hallucinogen that causes euphoria, paranoia, hallucinations, cognitive slowing, and conjunctival injection.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Marijuana is a hallucinogen that causes euphoria, anxiety, perception of slowed time, impaired judgement, increased appetite, and dry mouth. Cognitive slowing can occur, demonstrated in this patient by slow speech, prolonged response latency, and inability to perform serial sevens. Tachycardia and hypertension are likely to be seen on examination. Conjunctiva! injection is common in marijuana intoxication. Patients with marijuana intoxication can also present with paranoid delusions, social withdrawal, and hallucinations, such as in this patient.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, F, G, H, and I. Alcohol is a central nervous system depressant and intoxication (Choice A) can cause slurred speech, emotional !ability, behavioral disinhibition, ataxia, and memory lapses. In contrast, withdrawal (Choice B) is characterized by tremors, anxiety, restlessness, headache, nausea, and diaphoresis, which can occur hours after the cessation of alcohol use. Cocaine is a sympathomimetic drug that can cause euphoria, restlessness, tachycardia, and mydriasis during intoxication. Withdrawal (Choice C) can cause depressed mood, fatigue, and slowing of activity. Delusional disorder (Choice D) features one or more delusions for a month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. This patient does not have a fixed delusion and exhibits a general paranoia, making this diagnosis unlikely. Persecutory delusions are those in which a patient perceives others as attempting to punish them. Marijuana withdrawal (Choice F) can present with irritability, anxiety, depression, insomnia, and restlessness. This patient's speech is slow with prolonged response latency, making marijuana intoxication more likely. Panic disorder (Choice G) features panic attacks that occur unexpectedly and are associated with changes in behavior to avoid panic attack triggers. Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peaks within minutes and is associated with somatic symptoms such as palpitations, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, or gastrointestinal distress. Paranoid personality disorder (Choice H) is a cluster A personality disorder characterized by a pervasive mistrust of others and viewing others as possessing malicious intent. These patients tend to blame others for their own shortcomings. Personality disorders arise early in life, and this patient's new bizarre behavior is more likely caused by marijuana use. Patients with schizophrenia (Choice I) have chronic symptoms and demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, or alogia). This patient does have paranoia but is not disorganized and her presentation is acute, making schizophrenia unlikely.

questions.retrievalTag: 82-Year-Old Daughter Been Forgetful During Past Weeks Particularly
questions.stem: An 82-year-old man is brought to the physician by his daughter because he has been forgetful during the past 6 weeks. She is particularly concerned because a small fire started recently when he left a pot unattended on the stove. The patient's wife died 1 year ago, and he says he has been distracted recently and cannot stop thinking about her. He lives alone and pays his bills, does routine household chores, and drives short distances for errands. His daughter says he was doing well on his own until recently. He is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min and reg ular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is alert and cooperative. He becomes very sad when he talks about his wife's death. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: When patients present with subjective memory concerns, the first diagnostic step is a short cognitive screen such as the Mini-Mental State Examination. Once cognitive impairment is confirmed, thorough histories and examinations, depression screenings, laboratory studies, and neuroimaging should be obtained to diagnose the cause of cognitive impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: When patients or patients' family members report a subjective concern about the patient's memory, the first diagnostic step is to administer a short cognitive screen such as the Mini-Mental State Examination. This patient's current cognitive function appears decreased compared to his cognitive baseline of being able to execute his instrumental activities of daily living. The differential for cognitive impairment in an older individual is broad and includes primary dementia, pseudodementia secondary to major depressive disorder, and delirium (less likely given the patient's alertness on examination and the subacute, rather than acute, onset). Dementia can be caused by neurodegenerative proteinopathies (eg, Alzheimer dementia, Lewy body dementia, Parkinson dementia, or frontotemporal dementia), focal neurologic processes (eg, stroke or subdural hematoma), nutritional deficiencies (eg, vitamin B12), or endocrine disease (eg, hypothyroidism). All patients with confirmed cognitive impairment on cognitive screening should undergo thorough histories and physical examinations, depression screenings, laboratory workup to include vitamin B12 and thyroid function tests, and neuroimaging.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, C, D, and E. Follow-up examination in 3 months (Choice A) would be inappropriate without investigating the daughter's concern with a cognitive screening. Recommending assisted living (Choice C) may be indicated if the patient possesses dementia secondary to an irreversible cause. However, the physician has not yet ruled out reversible causes such as depression, thyroid dysfunction, or vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency. CT scan of the head (Choice D) would be indicated to evaluate for structural causes of cognitive impairment (eg, subdural hematoma) if screening is positive for cognitive impairment. Beginning donepezil therapy (Choice E) would be appropriate if the physician determines that the patient has dementia secondary to a neurodegenerative proteinopathy (eg, Alzheimer dementia, Lewy body dementia, or Parkinson dementia) or vascular dementia. Cognitive screening and diagnostic evaluation are the best initial steps.

questions.retrievalTag: 52-Year-Old Woman Routine Follow-Up Examination Received Inpatient Psychiatric
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. She has received inpatient psychiatric treatment several times since the age of 25 years for auditory hallucinations and the belief that her thoughts and movements were being controlled by a local television station; her last admission to the hospital was 10 years ago. Treatment with haloperidol for the past 20 years has decreased the occurrence of symptoms. She attempted suicide at the ages of 30 and 38 years. She lives in a supervised residence and does not work. During the examination, she repeatedly smacks her lips and slightly protrudes her tongue. When the physician asks if she is chewing gum, she laughs and opens her mouth to show she is not. She can hold her mouth and tongue still when asked but begins lip smacking when the physician resumes the examination. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Chronic use of typical antipsychotics (eg, haloperidol or chlorpromazine) can cause tardive dyskinesia, which presents with lip smacking and choreoathetoid tongue movements. Treatment involves discontinuing the typical antipsychotic and transitioning to an atypical antipsychotic, such as risperidone or olanzapine.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient with a history of auditory hallucinations, delusions, and suicide attempts likely has a diagnosis of schizophrenia. Patients with schizophrenia typically demonstrate clusters of positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized thinking and behavior) and negative symptoms (loss of affect, volition, or speech). Treatment for schizophrenia consists of antipsychotics, such as haloperidol, chlorpromazine, olanzapine, and risperidone. Use of typical antipsychotics, such as haloperidol or chlorpromazine, is associated with numerous adverse effects, such as acute dystonia, akathisia, bradykinesia or Parkinson-like effects, and tardive dyskinesia. Tardive dyskinesia potentially occurs with the long-term use of typical antipsychotics and is a syndrome of involuntary movements (eg, lip smacking or choreoathetoid movements of the tongue). Treatment involves discontinuing the typical antipsychotic and switching to an atypical antipsychotic, such as olanzapine or risperidone.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, and D. Continuing haloperidol and adding alprazolam (Choice A) would lead to worsening of the tardive dyskinesia because it is an adverse effect of the haloperidol use. Alprazolam and other benzodiazepines can be used to treat the akathisia associated with typical antipsychotics but do not treat tardive dyskinesia. Continuing haloperidol and adding propranolol (Choice B) would lead to worsening of the tardive dyskinesia because it is an adverse effect of the haloperidol use. Propranolol and other β-adrenergic blockers can be used to treat the akathisia associated with typical antipsychotics but do not treat tardive dyskinesia. Discontinuing haloperidol (Choice C) is appropriate in the setting of tardive dyskinesia. However, this patient has evidence of significant schizophrenia with previous suicide attempts and requires long-term treatment with antipsychotics. Failure to restart another antipsychotic in this patient may lead to a recurrent hospitalization or suicide attempt. Discontinuing haloperidol and beginning chlorpromazine (Choice D) would be ineffective in controlling this patient's tardive dyskinesia. Chlorpromazine is also a typical antipsychotic and, similar to haloperidol, could cause the tardive dyskinesia to continue to worsen.

questions.retrievalTag: 37-Year-Old Emergency Department After Pilot Boat Found River
questions.stem: A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after the pilot of a boat found him in the river at 2 am with rocks in his pockets. He reports that he jumped from the bridge to go for a swim and was not attempting suicide. He insists that the rocks were only to give him ballast when he jumped so that he would not hit the bridge tower. He immigrated to the USA 1 year ago and left most of his family behind. He has been living in a single furnished room and has been working numerous low-paying jobs. Over the past month, he has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss. He reports that he always feels tired but has not seen a physician because he has no health insurance. He does not usually drink but had two shots of whiskey earlier that night. He has no history of medical or psychiatric illness. Physical examination shows numerous lacerations and abrasions over the chest and all extremities. On mental status examination, he describes his mood as fine but often appears tearful. His speech is slow with frequent pauses, and he is guarded in his responses. He says that he has not had hallucinations. He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. The physician recommends voluntary admission to the psychiatric unit, but the patient refuses, saying there is nothing wrong with him. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Recommend a mental health facility in his neighborhood

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Observe in the emergency department for 36 hours

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Admit to the psychiatric unit involuntarily

questions.educationalObjective: When a patient possesses several serious acute suicide risk factors (eg, evidence of active suicidal intent, a recent suicide attempt, acute mood changes, or increased substance use), hospitalization should be pursued. The physician should ask the patient how they feel about being hospitalized, which determines whether the physician will pursue a voluntary or involuntary hospitalization.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient possesses several acute risk factors for suicide. Most concerningly, he was found after a likely suicide attempt Uumping off a bridge with rocks to go for a swim does not make logical sense). He denies that the incident was a suicide attempt and declines a psychiatric admission, which may indicate he still wishes to attempt suicide and is not willing or able to make a safety plan. Other acute suicide risk factors possessed by this patient include a depressive episode (as evidenced by weight loss indicating a decreased appetite, decreased energy, and tearful affect), social isolation, and increased substance use. Other acute suicide risk factors include acute anxiety, feeling like a burden, and command auditory hallucinations. In patients with several acute suicide risk factors, the physician should assess the patient's willingness to be hospitalized and pursue voluntary hospitalization if the patient is willing. In this scenario, if the patient declines hospitalization, the physician should involuntarily hospitalize the patient because of the probable presence of continued active suicidal ideation with intent and the patient's unwillingness to make a safety plan.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, and E. Recommending a mental health facility (Choice A) or social service agency (Choice B) may be appropriate after acute stabilization in the hospital. These options would be insufficient to prevent a repeat suicide attempt in this patient with an increased, acute suicide risk. Observing the patient in the emergency room for 36 hours (Choice C) would protect the patient from repeating a suicide attempt but would not be as effective as a psychiatric admission in addressing the patient's underlying psychiatric symptoms. Psychiatric admissions provide a therapeutic environment, including individual and group counseling, and the opportunity to initiate appropriate pharmacotherapy. Beginning fluoxetine therapy (Choice D), a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), may be appropriate in the next few days. However, SSRls take weeks to be effective, and the most acute issue is the patient's suicide risk. Beginning risperidone therapy (Choice E), an antipsychotic medication, would be inappropriate in this patient without clear psychotic symptoms. Though his explanation of the incident is illogical, the illogical nature is likely caused by the patient intentionally hiding his suicidal intent rather than a psychotic disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: 27-Year-Old Anxiety About Major Speech That Must Deliver
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of anxiety about a major speech that he must deliver in 3 days. He is excited about the opportunity for his career but is concerned that his apprehension and great fear of public speaking will affect his performance. He requests a tranquilizer to help with his anxiety. He has no history of medical problems. He has a family history of alcohol dependence. He does not drink alcohol or use drugs. His pulse is 76/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Examination shows normal findings.
questions.educationalObjective: Social anxiety disorder refers to excessive fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social or performance situations. In patients with social anxiety specific to performance situations, as-needed medications such as propranolol are commonly utilized.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Social anxiety disorder refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social or performance situations, leading to significant distress and/or impaired functioning. Patients with social anxiety disorder commonly exhibit shyness and performance anxiety. Performance anxiety refers to the fear of negative evaluation in situations such as public speaking, test-taking, and sex. Some patients with social anxiety disorder experience anxiety that is specific to performance situations only. In the performance-only subtype of social anxiety, cognitive-behavioral therapy is recommended as the first-line treatment, though as-needed anxiety medications are frequently utilized as well. In this patient with a family history of addiction, propranolol is preferred to benzodiazepine medication. Propranolol, a β-adrenergic blocker, reduces sympathetic activation associated with performance situations.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and H. Bupropion (Choice A) is an antidepressant medication that increases synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine. Fluoxetine (Choice D) is an antidepressant medication that is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor. Imipramine (Choice F) is a tricyclic antidepressant. These antidepressant medications take several weeks to work, making them less preferable for this patient. Further, these medications are typically utilized on a long- term basis for patients with frequent anxiety. Patients with infrequent anxiety can be managed with as-needed anxiety medication such as propranolol. Buspirone (Choice B) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. Buspirone takes 1 to 2 weeks to start working and is ineffective as an as-needed medication for performance anxiety. Chlorpromazine (Choice C), haloperidol (Choice E), and perphenazine (Choice H) are typical antipsychotics utilized in schizophrenia spectrum disorders. Given the risk for serious adverse effects and a lack of efficacy data, antipsychotic medications are not typically utilized for anxiety disorders. Lorazepam (Choice G) is a benzodiazepine medication utilized to treat anxiety, convulsions, and alcohol withdrawal. Benzodiazepine medications carry a risk for addiction and should be avoided in patients with a personal or family history of addiction if other effective treatments exist.

sharedGroup.sharedStem: For each patient with anxiety, select the most appropriate pharmacotherapy.

questions.retrievalTag: 27-Year-Old Anxiety About Major Speech That Must Deliver
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of anxiety about a major speech that he must deliver in 3 days. He has a great fear of public speaking and is convinced that his apprehension and tremulous delivery will damage his performance. He requests a tranquilizer to help with his anxiety. He takes theophylline and uses corticosteroid and albuterol inhalers for asthma. He has a family history of alcohol dependence. He does not drink alcohol or use drugs. His pulse is 66/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 132/80 mm Hg. Examination shows normal findings.
questions.educationalObjective: Social anxiety disorder refers to excessive fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social or performance situations. In patients with social anxiety specific to performance situations, as-needed medications such as propranolol and benzodiazepine medications are commonly utilized. In patients with asthma, benzodiazepine medications should be used because propranolol can potentially cause bronchoconstriction.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Social anxiety disorder refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social or performance situations, leading to significant distress and/or impaired functioning. Patients with social anxiety disorder commonly exhibit shyness and performance anxiety. Performance anxiety refers to the fear of negative evaluation in situations such as public speaking, test-taking, and sex. Some patients with social anxiety disorder experience anxiety that is specific to performance situations only. In the performance-only subtype of social anxiety, cognitive-behavioral therapy is recommended as the first-line treatment, though as-needed anxiety medications are frequently utilized as well. The most commonly used as-needed medications are propranolol and short-acting benzodiazepine medications (eg, lorazepam). In patients with asthma, propranolol's β 2 antagonism can potentially cause bronchoconstriction and consequent asthma exacerbation (and blocks the bronchodilatory effect of albuterol). Therefore, lorazepam should be prescribed instead.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, C, D, E, F, H, and I. Bupropion (Choice A) is an antidepressant medication that increases synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine. Fluoxetine (Choice D) is an antidepressant medication that is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor. Imipramine (Choice F) is a tricyclic antidepressant. These antidepressant medications take several weeks to work, making them less preferable for this patient. Further, these medications are typically utilized on a long- term basis for patients with frequent anxiety. Patients with infrequent anxiety can be managed with as-needed anxiety medication such as propranolol or a short-acting benzodiazepine medication. Buspirone (Choice B) is a serotonin modulator utilized to treat generalized anxiety disorder. Buspirone takes 1 to 2 weeks to start working and is ineffective as an as-needed medication for performance anxiety. Chlorpromazine (Choice C), haloperidol (Choice E), and perphenazine (Choice H) are typical antipsychotics utilized in schizophrenia spectrum disorders. Given the risk for serious adverse effects and a lack of efficacy data, antipsychotic medications are not typically utilized for anxiety disorders. Propranolol (Choice I) is a commonly utilized medication for performance anxiety. However, non-selective β-adrenergic blockers should be avoided in patients with asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

questions.retrievalTag: 8-Month-Old Male Infant Adopted Recently Poor Weight Gain
questions.stem: An 8-month-old male infant who was adopted recently is brought to the physician because of poor weight gain. He was born at term and weighed 2410 g (5 lb 5 oz). He was formula fed at birth and has consistently been below the 3rd percentile for weight. Currently, his diet consists of 16 ounces of iron-fortified cow milk formula, juice, cereal, prepared fruit, and pureed green vegetables. There is no history of vomiting. He has one formed stool daily. He is alert and appears well. He currently weighs 7002 g (15 lb 7 oz). On examination, he says "mama" and "bye-bye" while waving. He sits unsupported. He is wary of strangers. There are narrow palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, a thin upper lip with a "fish mouth" appearance of the oral cavity, and an indistinct nasal philtrum. His ears are normally set, the nasal bridge is flattened, and the tongue is not enlarged. There is a single palmar crease on the left hand. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. There is no hepatosplenomegaly, and no masses are palpated. There is no peripheral edema. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol is a known teratogen that causes fetal alcohol syndrome when used during pregnancy. Fetal alcohol syndrome presents with physical malformations such as microcephaly, facial dysmorphia, limb deformities, and congenital heart disease, along with intellectual disability and developmental delay.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Alcohol is a proven teratogen, as alcohol use during pregnancy leads to irreversible fetal neurologic effects and physical malformations in a cluster of symptoms called fetal alcohol syndrome. Because of the failure of cell migration, fetal alcohol syndrome presents with physical abnormalities such as microcephaly, facial abnormalities (small palpebral fissures, thin vermilion border, cleft lip/palate, or smooth or long philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease. Patients with fetal alcohol syndrome may demonstrate abnormal bone development (eg, cervical spine fusion) and delayed bone growth. Though alcohol is associated with obstetric complications such as preterm birth and low birth weight that commonly result from the use of substances during pregnancy, the physical malformations, including those seen in this case, are specific to alcohol use. Growth after birth may be restricted because of eating difficulties caused by the neurodevelopmental effects of alcohol. Fetal alcohol syndrome is a leading cause of intellectual disability in the United States, though this patient is meeting milestones appropriately to this point.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: A, B, D, and E. Celiac disease (Choice A) is a gluten-sensitive enteropathy that causes inflammatory infiltration of the proximal small bowel, which leads to the malabsorption. This presents symptomatically as chronic diarrhea, steatorrhea, and nutritional deficiencies. While it may be a cause of decreased weight gain in children, it is not associated with changes to facial features. Characteristic physical findings of Down syndrome (Choice B) include intellectual disability, broad, flat, facial features with prominent epicanthal folds, and a single palmar crease. There are similarities between the abnormal facies of patients with fetal alcohol syndrome and Down syndrome, but this patient's combination of growth retardation and characteristic physical features are most suggestive of fetal alcohol syndrome. Psychosocial deprivation (Choice D) or other psychosocial stressors are a prominent cause of growth delay in infants and children. Stressors including poverty, food insecurity, spousal discord, alcohol or substance abuse, or maternal abuse may all impact the growth of the child. None of these would explain this patient's dysmorphic appearance, however. Silver-Russell syndrome (Choice E) is one genetic cause of short stature caused by an epigenetic error in the expression of the insulin- like growth factor 2 gene on chromosome 11. Clinical features include intrauterine and postnatal growth restriction and characteristic facial features such as a triangular face, downturned mouth, and hemihypertrophy of the limbs. Feeding difficulties and developmental delay may be present. Thus, while similar to fetal alcohol syndrome, the two can be distinguished on the basis of facial features.
